title: The Two Voices
content:
A still small voice spake unto me,
ï¿½Thou art so full of misery,
Were it not better not to be?ï¿½

Then to the still small voice I said;
ï¿½Let me not cast in endless shade
What is so wonderfully made.ï¿½

To which the voice did urge reply;
ï¿½To-day I saw the dragon-fly
Come from the wells where he did lie.

ï¿½An inner impulse rent the veil
Of his old husk: from head to tail
Came out clear plates of sapphire mail.

ï¿½He dried his wings: like gauze they grew;
Throï¿½ crofts and pastures wet with dew
A living flash of light he flew.ï¿½

I said, ï¿½When first the world began,
Young Nature throï¿½ five cycles ran,
And in the sixth she moulded man.

ï¿½She gave him mind, the lordliest
Proportion, and, above the rest,
Dominion in the head and breast.ï¿½

Thereto the silent voice replied;
ï¿½Self-blinded are you by your pride:
Look up throï¿½ night: the world is wide.

ï¿½This truth within thy mind rehearse,
That in a boundless universe
Is boundless better, boundless worse.

ï¿½Think you this mould of hopes and fears
Could find no statelier than his peers
In yonder hundred million spheres?ï¿½

It spake, moreover, in my mind:
ï¿½Thoï¿½ thou wert scatterï¿½d to the wind,
Yet is there plenty of the kind.ï¿½

Then did my response clearer fall:
ï¿½No compound of this earthly ball
Is like another, all in all.ï¿½

To which he answerï¿½d scoffingly;
ï¿½Good soul! suppose I grant it thee,
Whoï¿½ll weep for thy deficiency?

ï¿½Or will one beam be less intense,
When thy peculiar differenee
Is cancellï¿½d in the world of sense?ï¿½

I would have said, ï¿½Thou canst not know,ï¿½
But my full heart, that workï¿½d below,
Rainï¿½d throï¿½ my sight its overflow.

Again the voice spake unto me:
ï¿½Thou art so steepï¿½d in misery,
Surely ï¿½twere better not to be.

ï¿½Thine anguish will not let thee sleep,
Nor any train of reason keep:
Thou canst not think, but thou wilt weep.ï¿½

I said, ï¿½The years with change advance:
If I make dark my countenance,
I shut my life from happier chance.

ï¿½Some turn this sickness yet might take,
Evï¿½n yet.ï¿½ But he: ï¿½What drug can make
A witherï¿½d palsy cease to shake?ï¿½

I wept, ï¿½Thoï¿½ I should die, I know
That all about the thorn will blow
In tufts of rosy-tinted snow;

ï¿½And men, throï¿½ novel spheres of thought
Still moving after truth long sought,
Will learn new things when I am not.ï¿½

ï¿½Yet,ï¿½ said the secret voice, ï¿½some time,
Sooner or later, will gray prime
Make thy grass hoar with early rime.

ï¿½Not less swift souls that yearn for light,
Rapt after heavenï¿½s starry flight,
Would sweep the tracts of day and night.

ï¿½Not less the bee would range her cells,
The furzy prickle fire the dells,
The foxglove cluster dappled bells.ï¿½

I said that ï¿½all the years invent;
Each month is various to present
The world with some development.

ï¿½Were this not well, to bide mine hour,
Thoï¿½ watching from a ruinï¿½d tower
How grows the day of human power?ï¿½

ï¿½The highest-mounted mind,ï¿½ he said,
ï¿½Still sees the sacred morning spread
The silent summit overhead.

ï¿½Will thirty seasons render plain
Those lonely lights that still remain,
Just breaking over land and main?

ï¿½Or make that morn, from his cold crown
And crystal silence creeping down,
Flood with full daylight glebe and town?

ï¿½Forerun thy peers, thy time, and let
Thy feet, millenniums hence, be set
In midst of knowledge, dreamï¿½d not yet.

ï¿½Thou hast not gainï¿½d a real height,
Nor art thou nearer to the light,
Because the scale is infinite.

ï¿½ï¿½Twere better not to breathe or speak,
Than cry for strength, remaining weak,
And seem to find, but still to seek.

ï¿½Moreover, but to seem to find
Asks what thou lackest, thought resignï¿½d,
A healthy frame, a quiet mind.ï¿½

I said, ï¿½When I am gone away,
ï¿½He dared not tarry,ï¿½ men will say,
Doing dishonour to my clay.ï¿½

ï¿½This is more vile,ï¿½ he made reply,
ï¿½To breathe and loathe, to live and sigh,
Than once from dread of pain to die.

ï¿½Sick art thouï¿½a divided will
Still heaping on the fear of ill
The fear of men, a coward still.

ï¿½Do men love thee? Art thou so bound
To men, that how thy name may sound
Will vex thee lying underground?

ï¿½The memory of the witherï¿½d leaf
In endless time is scarce more brief
Than of the garnerï¿½d Autumn-sheaf.

ï¿½Go, vexed Spirit, sleep in trust;
The right ear, that is fillï¿½d with dust,
Hears little of the false or just.ï¿½

ï¿½Hard task, to pluck resolve,ï¿½ I cried,
ï¿½From emptiness and the waste wide
Of that abyss, or scornful pride!

ï¿½Nayï¿½rather yet that I could raise
One hope that warmï¿½d me in the days
While still I yearnï¿½d for human praise.

ï¿½When, wide in soul and bold of tongue,
Among the tents I paused and sung,
The distant battle flashï¿½d and rung.

ï¿½I sung the joyful Pï¿½an clear,
And, sitting, burnishï¿½d without fear
The brand, the buckler, and the spearï¿½

ï¿½Waiting to strive a happy strife,
To war with falsehood to the knife,
And not to lose the good of lifeï¿½

ï¿½Some hidden principle to move,
To put together, part and prove,
And mete the bounds of hate and loveï¿½

ï¿½As far as might be, to carve out
Free space for every human doubt,
That the whole mind might orb aboutï¿½

ï¿½To search throï¿½ all I felt or saw,
The springs of life, the depths of awe,
And reach the law within the law:

ï¿½At least, not rotting like a weed,
But, having sown some generous seed,
Fruitful of further thought and deed,

ï¿½To pass, when Life her light withdraws,
Not void of righteous self-applause,
Nor in a merely selfish causeï¿½

ï¿½In some good cause, not in mine own,
To perish, wept for, honourï¿½d, known,
And like a warrior overthrown;

ï¿½Whose eyes are dim with glorious tears,
When, soilï¿½d with noble dust, he hears
His countryï¿½s war-song thrill his ears:

ï¿½Then dying of a mortal stroke,
What time the foemanï¿½s line is broke,
And all the war is rollï¿½d in smoke.ï¿½

ï¿½Yea!ï¿½ said the voice, ï¿½thy dream was good,
While thou abodest in the bud.
It was the stirring of the blood.

ï¿½If Nature put not forth her power
About the opening of the flower,
Who is it that could live an hour?

ï¿½Then comes the check, the change, the fall,
Pain rises up, old pleasures pall.
There is one remedy for all.

ï¿½Yet hadst thou, throï¿½ enduring pain,
Linkï¿½d month to month with such a chain
Of knitted purport, all were vain.

ï¿½Thou hadst not between death and birth
Dissolved the riddle of the earth.
So were thy labour little-worth.

ï¿½That men with knowledge merely playï¿½d,
I told theeï¿½hardly nigher made,
Thoï¿½ scaling slow from grade to grade;

ï¿½Much less this dreamer, deaf and blind,
Named man, may hope some truth to find,
That bears relation to the mind.

ï¿½For every worm beneath the moon
Draws different threads, and late and soon
Spins, toiling out his own cocoon.

ï¿½Cry, faint not: either Truth is born
Beyond the polar gleam forlorn,
Or in the gateways of the morn.

ï¿½Cry, faint not, climb: the summits slope
Beyond the furthest flights of hope,
Wrapt in dense cloud from base to cope.

ï¿½Sometimes a little corner shines,
As over rainy mist inclines
A gleaming crag with belts of pines.

ï¿½I will go forward, sayest thou,
I shall not fail to find her now.
Look up, the fold is on her brow.

ï¿½If straight thy track, or if oblique,
Thou knowï¿½st not. Shadows thou dost strike,
Embracing cloud, Ixion-like;

ï¿½And owning but a little more
Than beasts, abidest lame and poor,
Calling thyself a little lower

ï¿½Than angels. Cease to wail and brawl!
Why inch by inch to darkness crawl?
There is one remedy for all.ï¿½

ï¿½O dull, one-sided voice,ï¿½ said I,
ï¿½Wilt thou make everything a lie,
To flatter me that I may die?

ï¿½I know that age to age succeeds,
Blowing a noise of tongues and deeds,
A dust of systems and of creeds.

ï¿½I cannot hide that some have striven,
Achieving calm, to whom was given
The joy that mixes man with Heaven:

ï¿½Who, rowing hard against the stream,
Saw distant gates of Eden gleam,
And did not dream it was a dream;

ï¿½But heard, by secret transport led,
Evï¿½n in the charnels of the dead,
The murmur of the fountain-headï¿½

ï¿½Which did accomplish their desire,
Bore and forebore, and did not tire,
Like Stephen, an unquenched fire.

ï¿½He heeded not reviling tones,
Nor sold his heart to idle moans,
Thoï¿½ cursed and scornï¿½d, and bruised with stones:

ï¿½But looking upward, full of grace,
He prayï¿½d, and from a happy place
God's glory smote him on the face.ï¿½

The sullen answer slid betwixt:
ï¿½Not that the grounds of hope were fixï¿½d,
The elements were kindlier mixï¿½d.ï¿½

I said, ï¿½I toil beneath the curse,
But, knowing not the universe,
I fear to slide from bad to worse.

ï¿½And that, in seeking to undo
One riddle, and to find the true,
I knit a hundred others new:

ï¿½Or that this anguish fleeting hence,
Unmanacled from bonds of sense,
Be fixï¿½d and frozï¿½n to permanence:

ï¿½For I go, weak from suffering here:
Naked I go, and void of cheer:
What is it that I may not fear?ï¿½

ï¿½Consider well,ï¿½ the voice replied,
ï¿½His face, that two hours since hath died;
Wilt thou find passion, pain or pride?

ï¿½Will he obey when one commands?
Or answer should one press his hands?
He answers not, nor understands.

ï¿½His palms are folded on his breast:
There is no other thing expressï¿½d
But long disquiet merged in rest.

ï¿½His lips are very mild and meek:
Thoï¿½ one should smite him on the cheek,
And on the mouth, he will not speak.

ï¿½His little daughter, whose sweet face
He kissï¿½d, taking his last embrace,
Becomes dishonour to her raceï¿½

ï¿½His sons grow up that bear his name,
Some grow to honour, some to shame,ï¿½
But he is chill to praise or blame.

ï¿½He will not hear the north-wind rave,
Nor, moaning, household shelter crave
From winter rains that beat his grave.

ï¿½High up the vapours fold and swim:
About him broods the twilight dim:
The place he knew forgetteth him.ï¿½

ï¿½If all he dark, vague voice,ï¿½ I said,
ï¿½These things are wrapt in doubt and dread,
Nor canst thou show the dead are dead.

ï¿½The sap dries up: the plant declines.
A deeper tale my heart divines.
Know I not Death? the outward signs?

ï¿½I found him when my years were few;
A shadow on the graves I knew,
And darkness in the village yew.

ï¿½From grave to grave the shadow crept:
In her still place the morning wept:
Touchï¿½d by his feet the daisy slept.

ï¿½The simple senses crownï¿½d his head:
ï¿½Omega! thou art Lord,ï¿½ they said,
ï¿½We find no motion in the dead.ï¿½

ï¿½Why, if man rot in dreamless ease,
Should that plain fact, as taught by these,
Not make him sure that he shall cease?

ï¿½Who forged that other influence,
That heat of inward evidence,
By which he doubts against the sense?

ï¿½He owns the fatal gift of eyes,
That read his spirit blindly wise,
Not simple as a thing that dies.

ï¿½Here sits he shaping wings to fly:
His heart forebodes a mystery:
He names the name Eternity.

ï¿½That type of Perfect in his mind
In Nature can he nowhere find.
He sows himself on every wind.

ï¿½He seems to hear a Heavenly Friend,
And throï¿½ thick veils to apprehend
A labour working to an end.

ï¿½The end and the beginning vex
His reason: many things perplex,
With motions, checks, and counterchecks.

ï¿½He knows a baseness in his blood
At such strange war with something good,
He may not do the thing he would.

ï¿½Heaven opens inward, chasms yawn,
Vast images in glimmering dawn,
Half shown, are broken and withdrawn.

ï¿½Ah! sure within him and without,
Could his dark wisdom find it out,
There must be answer to his doubt,

ï¿½But thou canst answer not again.
With thine own weapon art thou slain,
Or thou wilt answer but in vain.

ï¿½The doubt would rest, I dare not solve.
In the same circle we revolve.
Assurance only breeds resolve.ï¿½

As when a billow, blown against,
Falls back, the voice with which I fenced
A little ceased, but recommenced.

ï¿½Where wert thou when thy father playï¿½d
In his free field, and pastime made,
A merry boy in sun and shade?

ï¿½A merry boy they callï¿½d him then,
He sat upon the knees of men
In days that never come again.

ï¿½Before the little ducts began
To feed thy bones with lime, and ran
Their course, till thou wert also man:

ï¿½Who took a wife, who rearï¿½d his race,
Whose wrinkles gatherï¿½d on his face,
Whose troubles number with his days:

ï¿½A life of nothings, nothing-worth,
From that first nothing ere his birth
To that last nothing under earth!ï¿½

ï¿½These words,ï¿½ I said, ï¿½are like the rest;
No certain clearness, but at best
A vague suspicion of the breast:

ï¿½But if I grant, thou mightst defend
The thesis which thy words intendï¿½
That to begin implies to end;

ï¿½Yet how should I for certain hold,
Because my memory is so cold,
That I first was in human mould?

ï¿½I cannot make this matter plain,
But I would shoot, howeï¿½er in vain,
A random arrow from the brain.

ï¿½It may be that no life is found,
Which only to one engine bound
Falls off, but cycles always round.

ï¿½As old mythologies relate,
Some draught of Lethe might await
The slipping throï¿½ from state to state.

ï¿½As here we find in trances, men
Forget the dream that happens then,
Until they fall in trance again.

ï¿½So might we, if our state were such
As one before, remember much,
For those two likes might meet and touch.

ï¿½But, if I lapsed from nobler place,
Some legend of a fallen race
Alone might hint of my disgrace;

ï¿½Some vague emotion of delight
In gazing up an Alpine height,
Some yeaming toward the lamps of night;

ï¿½Or if throï¿½ lower lives I cameï¿½
Thoï¿½ all experience past became
Consolidate in mind and frameï¿½

ï¿½I might forget my weaker lot;
For is not our first year forgot?
The haunts of memory echo not.

ï¿½And men, whose reason long was blind,
From cells of madness unconfined,
Oft lose whole years of darker mind.

ï¿½Much more, if first I floated free,
As naked essence, must I be
Incompetent of memory:

ï¿½For memory dealing but with time,
And he with matter, could she climb
Beyond her own material prime?

ï¿½Moreover, something is or seems,
That touches me with mystic gleams,
Like glimpses of forgotten dreamsï¿½

ï¿½Of something felt, like something here;
Of something done, I know not where;
Such as no language may declare.ï¿½

The still voice laughï¿½d. ï¿½I talk,ï¿½ said he,
ï¿½Not with thy dreams. Suffice it thee
Thy pain is a reality.ï¿½

ï¿½But thou,ï¿½ said I, ï¿½hast missed thy mark,
Who soughtï¿½st to wreck my mortal ark,
By making all the horizon dark.

ï¿½Why not set forth, if I should do
This rashness, that which might ensue
With this old soul in organs new?

ï¿½Whatever crazy sorrow saith,
No life that breathes with human breath
Has ever truly longï¿½d for death.

ï¿½ï¿½Tis life, whereof our nerves are scant,
Oh life, not death, for which we pant;
More life, and fuller, that I want.ï¿½

I ceased, and sat as one forlorn.
Then said the voice, in quiet scorn,
ï¿½Behold, it is the Sabbath morn.ï¿½

And I arose, and I released
The casement, and the light increased
With freshness in the dawning east.

Like softenï¿½d airs that blowing steal,
When meres begin to uncongeal,
The sweet church bells began to peal.

On to Godï¿½s house the people prest:
Passing the place where each must rest,
Each enterï¿½d like a welcome guest.

One walkï¿½d between his wife and child,
With measured footfall firm and mild,
And now and then he gravely smiled.

The prudent partner of his blood
Leanï¿½d on him, faithful, gentle, good,
Wearing the rose of womanhood.

And in their double love secure,
The little maiden walkï¿½d demure,
Pacing with downward eyelids pure.

These three made unity so sweet,
My frozen heart began to beat,
Remembering its ancient heat.

I blest them, and they wanderï¿½d on:
I spoke, but answer came there none:
The dull and bitter voice was gone.

A second voice was at mine ear,
A little whisper silver-clear,
A murmur, ï¿½Be of better cheer.ï¿½

As from some blissful neighbourhood,
A notice faintly understood,
ï¿½I see the end, and know the good.ï¿½

A little hint to solace woe,
A hint, a whisper breathing low,
ï¿½I may not speak of what I know.ï¿½

Like an ï¿½olian harp that wakes
No certain air, but overtakes
Far thought with music that it makes:

Such seemï¿½d the whisper at my side:
ï¿½What is it thou knowest, sweet voice?ï¿½ I cried.
ï¿½A hidden hope,ï¿½ the voice replied:

So heavenly-toned, that in that hour
From out my sullen heart a power
Broke, like the rainbow from the shower,

To feel, althoï¿½ no tongue can prove,
That every cloud, that spreads above
And veileth love, itself is love.

And forth into the fields I went,
And Natureï¿½s living motion lent
The pulse of hope to discontent.

I wonderï¿½d at the bounteous hours,
The slow result of winter showers:
You scarce could see the grass for flowers.

I wonderï¿½d, while I paced along:
The woods were fillï¿½d so full with song,
There seemï¿½d no room for sense of wrong;

And all so variously wrought,
I marvellï¿½d how the mind was brought
To anchor by one gloomy thought;

And wherefore rather I made choice
To commune with that barren voice,
Than him that said, ï¿½Rejoice! Rejoice!ï¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
